episodes.title: Learning Brief — May 18, 2026

ai_news.title: Google's I/O Positioning, NVIDIA's Video Generation Tools, and the Agent Evaluation Shift
points: Google is entering its annual I/O developer conference positioned as a clear third player in the foundation model race, signaling a strategic recalibration in how it's competing against OpenAI and Anthropic in the AI landscape.

NVIDIA released Cosmos Predict 2.5 with LoRA and DoRA fine-tuning capabilities, enabling developers to customize video generation models for specific domains like robotics without full retraining—lowering the barrier to building specialized video AI products.

IBM Research and collaborators launched the Open Agent Leaderboard, establishing standardized benchmarks for evaluating agentic AI systems, addressing a critical gap in how teams can objectively compare agent architectures and performance.
product_implications: Google's third-place positioning matters for your build-versus-buy calculus. If Google is doubling down on developer accessibility and pricing at I/O, it could shift the competitive dynamics for embedding foundation models—watch for API pricing, context windows, and latency improvements that might make Google a viable alternative to your current vendor lock-in.

NVIDIA's fine-tuning tooling directly affects your robotics or video-heavy product roadmap. If you're building domain-specific video generation features, LoRA/DoRA means you can now customize models with smaller datasets and lower compute costs. This is a build-not-buy signal for teams with specialized video use cases and enough data to justify the effort.

The Open Agent Leaderboard solves a real PM problem: you finally have a common language for evaluating whether your agentic features actually work. If you're shipping agents to users, this leaderboard gives you benchmarks to track against competitors and to justify investment in agent quality to stakeholders—it's the first real measurement framework for agent reliability.

pm_news.title: Anthropic's Claude Code Shift: HTML Over Markdown Signals How AI Agents Are Changing Product Building
pm_news.content:
Anthropic engineers just shared something subtle but telling about how they're building with Claude Code: they've moved from markdown to HTML as the primary interface language for AI-generated specs and micro-apps. On the surface, that's a technical choice. But there's a real PM lesson buried in there.

Here's what's happening. When you're building tools for AI agents to use—not humans—the interface language matters differently than it does for documentation. Markdown was designed for human readability and portability. HTML gives you semantic structure, styling, and interactivity that AI models can actually parse and act on more reliably. It's not about what's easier for us to read. It's about what creates the most actionable output for the next agent in the chain.

This is a canary in the coal mine for product teams. As AI becomes embedded deeper into your workflows, you're going to face similar decisions: do you optimize your data structures, APIs, and outputs for human consumption, or for machine consumption? The answer increasingly is: both, but in different layers.

What Anthropic is signaling is that they're building a product ecosystem where AI agents are first-class citizens in the workflow. They're not bolting AI onto existing human-centric tools. They're rethinking the primitives from the ground up—choosing formats that let Claude Code generate, iterate, and hand off work to the next step with minimal friction.

If you're a senior PM thinking about AI-native products or how to integrate AI deeper into existing workflows, this is the mindset shift to watch. It's not "how do we make AI explain things better to humans?" It's "how do we make our product structure native to how AI agents actually work?" That's where the competitive advantage sits right now.

pm_learning.title: HTML is the new Markdown: How AI tooling changes what you build with
pm_learning.content:
Here's the thing that matters for how you think about AI products going forward: the medium you build in shapes what becomes possible, and that medium changes faster than most PMs realize.

Thariq Shihipar from Anthropic makes a deceptively simple observation — HTML replaced Markdown as the native format for Claude Code interactions. On the surface, that's a technical detail. But what that means in practice is that engineers stopped thinking about text outputs and started thinking about interactive interfaces. The constraint changed. And when constraints change, what people build changes with it.

This is the mental model worth sitting with: your product's affordances don't just enable features, they reshape what problems your users think they can solve. When the tool shifted from "here's text you read" to "here's an interactive spec you can edit live," the entire category of problems that felt solvable expanded. Suddenly you're not managing outputs — you're managing micro-applications.

Why does this matter to you as a PM? Because you're probably still thinking about AI features as "better search" or "smarter classification" or "faster writing." But the engineers who are winning aren't optimizing within the old medium. They're asking what becomes possible when you change the interface itself.

Think about it like this: Markdown forced a certain kind of thinking — linear, read-only, output-focused. HTML opens bidirectional interaction. That's not a small difference. That's the difference between a tool that generates and a tool that lets you iterate. The move here is to stop asking "how do I make AI better at X" and start asking "what interface would let my users do something they couldn't do before?"

This connects directly to how you prioritize. If you're measuring success by "did the AI output improve," you're optimizing for the wrong thing. You should be measuring whether the interface unlocked new behaviors. Did users start using this in ways we didn't predict? Are they solving problems they previously thought were impossible?

For this week: pick one AI feature you're currently shipping or planning. Don't ask how to make the model smarter. Instead, ask what interface change would make the output actually useful enough that people change their workflow. What would have to be true about the interaction layer — not the model — for this to matter?
pm_learning.mental_model: The interface you build in determines what problems become solvable; optimize for interface constraints that unlock new user behaviors, not just better outputs.

episodes.title: Learning Brief — May 19, 2026

ai_news.title: Open Embeddings Model Gains 32K Context; Chinese Short-Form Video Goes All-In on AI Generation
points: IBM released Granite Embedding Multilingual R2, an open-source embedding model under Apache 2.0 with 32K context window and sub-100M parameters. It's positioned as the best-in-class retrieval quality at that scale, available on Hugging Face.

Chinese short-drama platforms have scaled AI content generation to industrialize production pipelines, creating narrative-driven video at volumes that would be economically unfeasible with traditional crews. Studios are using AI for scripting, asset generation, and editing to feed massive demand.
product_implications: If you're building RAG or semantic search into your product, this changes the math on build-versus-buy for embeddings. A 32K context window means you can embed longer documents without chunking, reducing retrieval complexity and improving accuracy on dense queries. The open license removes vendor lock-in risk and lets you fine-tune on proprietary data without sharing it. This is table stakes for any PM shipping search or knowledge retrieval in the next 6 months.

The Chinese short-drama model is a real-time case study in how AI commoditizes content production at scale. If you're in media, entertainment, or any narrative-driven product, this signals that cost-per-unit-content is collapsing for certain formats. Your competitive moat shifts from production capacity to taste, curation, and audience insight. Watch this space if you're considering user-generated or AI-assisted content features — the economics just changed.

pm_news.title: Aakash Gupta's AI Prototyping Bakeoff: What PMs Should Learn From Real Tool Selection
pm_news.content:
Aakash Gupta just published something that feels rare in the PM world right now — an actual, transparent bakeoff of AI prototyping tools. Not a concept piece, not a listicle. A real comparison with screenshots, workflows, and a downloadable playbook. He tested multiple tools, picked a winner, and added it to his bundle.

Here's why this matters beyond just "oh, new tools exist." This is a case study in how you should be evaluating emerging technology for your own product organization. Gupta didn't just read marketing copy or watch demos. He ran the tools through realistic workflows, compared outputs side by side, and made a deliberate choice based on what actually works in practice.

For you as a senior PM, this is instructive on two fronts. First, it's a model for how to pressure-test AI tools before you commit your team's time and muscle memory to them. Second, it shows what transparency looks like when you're making a tool decision — he's showing his work, which means you can learn from his criteria and potentially replicate his methodology for tools relevant to your own context.

The broader pattern here is important too. We're in a phase where AI tool maturity is moving fast — Anthropic shipped 74 releases in 52 days, according to another piece in the same publication. That velocity means your evaluation process needs to be continuous and grounded in actual use, not theoretical capability. You can't rely on launch announcements or feature parity tables anymore.

The takeaway: when you're selecting tools or evaluating AI capabilities for your product roadmap, build in a real testing phase with your actual workflows before you standardize on anything.

pm_learning.title: Get to the Core of the Thing
pm_learning.content:
Here's the thing that separates senior PMs from the rest: the willingness to stop being comfortable and actually get specific about what's broken.

Shreyas Doshi's core insight is this — most of us default to abstract language when we're thinking through problems. We say things like "user engagement is low" or "our onboarding needs work" or "we should focus on retention." And then we move on. But that's not thinking. That's just restating the problem in different words.

The move here is to push yourself — and your team — to the hard specifics. What exact behaviour isn't happening? Which users? In what sequence? What's the measurable gap between what's happening now and what should happen? When you force yourself to answer those questions, the actual problem usually becomes obvious. And it's almost never what you thought it was.

Let's make this concrete. Imagine you're working on a B2B SaaS product and you say "our trial-to-paid conversion is too low." That's abstract. Now push deeper. Is it that people aren't coming back after day three? Or they're coming back but not inviting teammates? Or they're inviting teammates but those teammates aren't doing anything? Those are completely different problems with completely different solutions. The first one might be a content problem. The second is a collaboration problem. The third is a permissions or role clarity problem. You can't solve anything until you know which one it is.

What that means in practice is this: when you're in a planning meeting or a discovery conversation, and someone says something vague, your job is to ask the uncomfortable follow-up question. Not to be difficult, but because the specificity is where the insight lives. "What do you mean by that?" "Which users specifically?" "How many?" "What's the exact moment they stop?" "How do you know?" These aren't rhetorical questions. You're genuinely trying to narrow the aperture until you can see the actual problem, not the shadow of it.

This is especially important as you move into senior or group PM roles, because you're now responsible for making sure your teams aren't spinning on vague problems. You're the one who has to say "we're not going to prioritize this until we can describe what's actually broken."

Here's your action for this week: pick one active project or problem your team is working on. Write down how you'd currently describe it in a sentence. Then ask yourself — or your team — the uncomfortable questions until you can rewrite that description with actual numbers, specific user segments, and measurable gaps. That's the difference between thinking and talking about thinking.
pm_learning.mental_model: You haven't understood the problem until you can describe it with specifics — not abstractions.
